title: Feature-Market Fit — Museums, Historical Sites, and Similar Institutions (NAICS 712110)

sections.title: Step 09 — Technology-Level Feature-Market Fit
sections.content:
Technology-scope = features any product in the "Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guards" group delivers.  
Market needs grounded in JTBD error statements (E1–E19).

### Kano + ODI Scores

| Feature | Kano | Time | Cost | Safety | Rely. | Skill | Stress | Pain | Conf. | Delight | **Overall** |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Mechanical Handle Locking | `must_be` | 2 | 5 | 9 | 8 | 6 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 2 | **5.56** |
| Audible Alarm Trigger | `must_be` | 5 | 6 | 9 | 7 | 5 | 7 | 6 | 7 | 2 | **6.00** |
| Single-Motion Emergency Release | `must_be` | 3 | 3 | 10 | 8 | 8 | 6 | 4 | 7 | 2 | **5.67** |
| Key Override Bypass | `must_be` | 5 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 6 | 7 | 7 | 6 | 3 | **5.44** |
| Battery-Powered Operation | `attractive` | 7 | 7 | 6 | 5 | 8 | 6 | 9 | 5 | 7 | **6.67** |
| Pre-Alarm Warning | `attractive` | 4 | 5 | 4 | 4 | 5 | 7 | 6 | 5 | 5 | **5.00** |

### Rationale per Feature

**Mechanical Handle Locking — must_be, 5.56**  
Core commodity FP function; every product in the category delivers it. Primary drivers: Safety (9) — prevents unauthorized access to collection vaults and conservation storage, directly addressing E8 (imp=10, sat=3); Reliability (8) — mechanical fail-safe with no power dependency is essential in heritage buildings with unreliable mains. Delight (2) — table-stakes, no surprise value.

**Audible Alarm Trigger — must_be, 6.00**  
Commodity FP explicitly includes audible alarm; absence = product does not exist in category. Primary drivers: Safety (9) — 95 dB deters theft and alerts adjacent staff during opening hours; Stress (7) — security officers know any unauthorized activation is audible, reducing overnight anxiety. Slightly higher than Mechanical Handle Locking due to detection contribution.  
*E8 / E18 grounding.*

**Single-Motion Emergency Release — must_be, 5.67**  
Regulatory knockout without it (EN 179 / EN 1125 certification impossible). Primary drivers: Safety (10) — the core life-safety function; highest Safety score across all features; Skill (8) — requires no prior knowledge or technique from any visitor, including reduced-mobility museum visitors in historic buildings with unfamiliar layouts. Low Delight (2) — entirely expected.  
*E9 (imp=10, sat=8) is well addressed.*

**Key Override Bypass — must_be, 5.44**  
Institutionally required; absence makes the product inoperable for normal museum use. Primary drivers: Stress (7) and Pain (7) — conservation teams and night-shift security access storage areas without triggering false alerts (E10, imp=7); Pain eliminates unnecessary incident investigations from authorized movement. Safety (5) — neutral; key override does not enhance collection protection, it bypasses it.

**Battery-Powered Operation — attractive, 6.67**  
Highest overall fit. Not expected — wired alarm panels dominate institutional security; battery power creates disproportionate value by eliminating mains-wiring heritage consent. Primary drivers: Pain (9) — removes the 3–12 month listed building consent barrier that disqualifies Wired incumbent (A2) entirely in S2 heritage segments; Skill (8) — no electrician required, installation achievable by facility staff; Delight (7) — museum facilities managers are genuinely surprised by no-wiring-needed deployment.  
*E5 (imp=8) and E6 (imp=9) grounding. Heritage inversion: C5 + battery power are inseparable in this market.*

**Pre-Alarm Warning — attractive, 5.00**  
Absent in basic mechanical alarms; reduces false-alarm fatigue during opening hours. Primary drivers: Stress (7) — duty managers avoid unnecessary alarm investigations from accidental visitor contact (E10, E15); Pain (6) — reduces workload from minor door-touch incidents. Safety (4) receives a moderate score: pre-alarm may give an unauthorized user 1–2 seconds of warning in after-hours vault contexts, though the full 95 dB alarm still triggers immediately. Not a significant detriment but constrains the Safety ceiling.

---

sections.title: Step 10 — Product-Related Needs
sections.content:
Needs that exist **because** this technology uses 868 MHz wireless transmission, a 9V block battery, and surface-mount mechanics. Each passes the litmus test: a wired sensor system (A2), gallery attendants (A4), or a basic mechanical alarm (A1) would not generate these needs.

| # | Statement | Source Constraint | Affected Markets | Imp | Sat |
|---|---|---|---|:---:|:---:|
| N1 | Minimize the likelihood of 868 MHz signal degradation from heritage-grade masonry (stone walls >400mm, reinforced concrete) creating silent monitoring gaps on emergency exit devices exceeding 15 minutes — in buildings where signal loss is not visually apparent | Wireless Range Limitation (C3) | Museums / Historical Sites | 8.0 | 4.0 |
| N2 | Minimize the time required to pre-validate 868 MHz radio coverage through actual building wall materials before committing to gateway placement — preventing post-installation discovery of exit zones with insufficient signal strength | Wireless Range Limitation (C3) | Museums / Historical Sites | 7.0 | 4.0 |
| N3 | Minimize the likelihood of 9V block battery depletion in exit monitoring devices below operational threshold within the 2-year maintenance window — accounting for reduced electrochemical performance in climate-controlled conservation environments (15–20°C, 45–55% RH) | IoT Battery Life Floor (C4) | Museums / Historical Sites · Schools · Senior Care | 7.0 | 4.0 |
| N4 | Minimize the time required to achieve complete emergency exit monitoring coverage across all doors in a multi-room heritage building using exclusively surface-mount fixing methods — completing installation within a single operational day without disrupting gallery activities | Retrofit-Only Surface Mount (C5) | Museums / Historical Sites | 8.0 | 5.0 |
| N5 | Minimize the likelihood of EasyWave protocol incompatibility causing integration failure when the museum BMS or intruder detection panel uses BACnet, KNX, or IP-based protocols — requiring additional unplanned gateway hardware to achieve centralized monitoring | EasyWave Protocol Lock-In (C6) | Museums / Historical Sites · Commercial Office Buildings | 6.0 | 5.0 |

**5 product-related needs. All 5 pass litmus test.** N1/N2 trace to C3 (range); N3 to C4 (battery); N4 to C5 (surface mount); N5 to C6 (protocol). Every constraint generates at least one need.

---

sections.title: Step 11 — Vendor Feature-Market Fit
sections.content:
Vendor: **GfS / Assa Abloy**  
Best incumbent reference: **dormakaba SafeRoute** (wired electronic exit control, LON/BACnet, BMS-integrated) for IoT features; basic mechanical alarm panel for physical features.

| Feature | Kano | Vendor | Incumbent | Advantage | Rationale |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| 868 MHz EasyWave Radio Transmission | `attractive` | 7 | 4 | **superior** | Battery-powered wireless at EU-reserved 868 MHz enables heritage retrofit without wiring consent. dormakaba SafeRoute (best wired incumbent) scores low because mains wiring requires heritage consent in listed buildings — functionally disqualified in S2. EasyWave scores below theoretical optimum due to proprietary protocol (C6) and 30m range limit in large museums. |
| Wireless BUS Interface Integration | `attractive` | 5 | 7 | **inferior** | Proprietary GfS-only ecosystem with no native BACnet/KNX/IP support. dormakaba SafeRoute and GEZE SecuLogic provide superior multi-vendor BMS aggregation via open protocols. For S5 multi-site museum groups with centralized BMS, EasyWave lock-in (C6) limits scalability. Genuine weakness that must be addressed for large institutional group procurement. |
| Dual-Standard Variant Range (EN 179 + EN 1125) | `must_be` | 8 | 7 | **parity** | GfS matched EN 179 / EN 1125 families enable consistent deployment across mixed museum door configurations (lever-handle storage corridors + panic-bar public galleries). Most competitors offer dual-standard coverage; difference of 1 point = parity. |
| IP45 Outdoor Variant | `indifferent` | 5 | 5 | **parity** | Most museum exits are climate-controlled interior spaces. IP45 adds minor value only for outdoor courtyard doors or archaeological site perimeter points (S4). Standard indoor variant carries no published IP class. Market does not evaluate this feature for the dominant S1/S2 interior use case. |
| Glass-Frame Mounting Plate (Art. 991170) | `attractive` | 8 | 3 | **superior** | Modern museum additions, atria, and public-facing gallery extensions frequently use glass-frame internal doors. Art. 991170 enables no-modification installation on an otherwise uncoverable door type. No established competitor equivalent — alternatives require adhesive mounting or custom fabrication. Strong differentiator in procurement specifications that include glass-frame exit coverage. |

---

sections.title: Key Findings
sections.content:
| Dimension | Finding |
|---|---|
| **Strongest technology feature** | Battery-Powered Operation (6.67) — highest fit; uniquely unlocks heritage market via no-consent installation |
| **Must-be floor** | 4 of 6 technology features are must_be — they set the compliance and safety baseline before IoT differentiation is even evaluated |
| **Heritage pairing** | Battery-Powered Operation + surface-mount mechanics together resolve E5/E6 (heritage consent barrier) — no incumbent alternative replicates this pairing |
| **Vendor advantage** | Superior on wireless retrofit (EasyWave for heritage buildings) and glass-frame coverage (Art. 991170) |
| **Vendor gap** | Inferior on BMS integration — EasyWave protocol lock-in (C6) is a genuine liability for S1/S5 segments with centralized BMS |
| **Primary product-related risk** | N1 + N2: RF signal uncertainty in thick masonry; unresolved at point of sale; affects adoption in S1/S2 listed buildings |
| **Highest single opportunity** | E8 (vault activation undetected >2 min, OS=17): EasyWave radio + battery operation address this need directly with no wired equivalent viable in heritage buildings |

---

rows.Statement: Minimize the likelihood of 868 MHz signal degradation from heritage-grade masonry (stone walls >400mm, reinforced concrete) creating silent monitoring gaps on emergency exit devices exceeding 15 minutes — in buildings where signal loss is not visually apparent

Minimize the time required to pre-validate 868 MHz radio coverage through actual building wall materials before committing to gateway placement — preventing post-installation discovery of exit zones with insufficient signal strength

Minimize the likelihood of 9V block battery depletion in exit monitoring devices below operational threshold within the 2-year maintenance window — accounting for reduced electrochemical performance in climate-controlled conservation environments (15–20°C, 45–55% RH)
rows.Affected Markets: Museums / Historical Sites · Schools · Senior Care

rows.Statement: Minimize the time required to achieve complete emergency exit monitoring coverage across all doors in a multi-room heritage building using exclusively surface-mount fixing methods — completing installation within a single operational day without disrupting gallery activities

Minimize the likelihood of EasyWave protocol incompatibility causing integration failure when the museum BMS or intruder detection panel uses BACnet, KNX, or IP-based protocols — requiring additional unplanned gateway hardware to achieve centralized monitoring
rows.Affected Markets: Museums / Historical Sites · Commercial Office Buildings

rows.Rationale: Battery-powered wireless at EU-reserved 868 MHz enables heritage retrofit without wiring consent. dormakaba SafeRoute (best wired incumbent) scores low because mains wiring requires heritage consent in listed buildings — functionally disqualified in S2. EasyWave scores below theoretical optimum due to proprietary protocol (C6) and 30m range limit in large museums.

Proprietary GfS-only ecosystem with no native BACnet/KNX/IP support. dormakaba SafeRoute and GEZE SecuLogic provide superior multi-vendor BMS aggregation via open protocols. For S5 multi-site museum groups with centralized BMS, EasyWave lock-in (C6) limits scalability. Genuine weakness that must be addressed for large institutional group procurement.

GfS matched EN 179 / EN 1125 families enable consistent deployment across mixed museum door configurations (lever-handle storage corridors + panic-bar public galleries). Most competitors offer dual-standard coverage; difference of 1 point = parity.

Most museum exits are climate-controlled interior spaces. IP45 adds minor value only for outdoor courtyard doors or archaeological site perimeter points (S4). Standard indoor variant carries no published IP class. Market does not evaluate this feature for the dominant S1/S2 interior use case.

Modern museum additions, atria, and public-facing gallery extensions frequently use glass-frame internal doors. Art. 991170 enables no-modification installation on an otherwise uncoverable door type. No established competitor equivalent — alternatives require adhesive mounting or custom fabrication. Strong differentiator in procurement specifications that include glass-frame exit coverage.

rows.Finding: Battery-Powered Operation (6.67) — highest fit; uniquely unlocks heritage market via no-consent installation
4 of 6 technology features are must_be — they set the compliance and safety baseline before IoT differentiation is even evaluated
Battery-Powered Operation + surface-mount mechanics together resolve E5/E6 (heritage consent barrier) — no incumbent alternative replicates this pairing
Superior on wireless retrofit (EasyWave for heritage buildings) and glass-frame coverage (Art. 991170)
Inferior on BMS integration — EasyWave protocol lock-in (C6) is a genuine liability for S1/S5 segments with centralized BMS
N1 + N2: RF signal uncertainty in thick masonry; unresolved at point of sale; affects adoption in S1/S2 listed buildings
E8 (vault activation undetected >2 min, OS=17): EasyWave radio + battery operation address this need directly with no wired equivalent viable in heritage buildings

entries.rationale: GfS matched EN 179/EN 1125 families enable consistent mixed-door deployment (lever-handle storage corridors + panic-bar public galleries). Most competitors offer dual-standard coverage. Difference of 1 = parity per classification rules.

entries.market_name: Museums, Historical Sites, and Similar Institutions
entries.rationale: Most museum exits are climate-controlled interior spaces. IP45 adds value only for outdoor courtyard doors or S4 archaeological sites — not the dominant S1/S2 interior use case. Standard indoor variant has no published IP class; market does not evaluate on this dimension.

entries.market_name: Museums, Historical Sites, and Similar Institutions
entries.rationale: Modern museum additions and public-facing galleries frequently use glass-frame internal doors. Art. 991170 enables no-modification installation on an otherwise uncoverable door type. No established competitor equivalent — alternatives require adhesive mounting or custom fabrication. Strong procurement differentiator when glass-frame exits must be included in coverage.
